title: Electric Power Sector Reform Act 2005
issuer: Federal Republic of Nigeria — National Assembly
key_promises: Unbundle NEPA into generation, transmission, distribution and trading successor companies
Establish Nigerian Electricity Regulatory Commission (NERC) as independent regulator
Introduce competitive electricity market in two phases
Establish Rural Electrification Agency and Rural Electrification Fund
Establish Power Consumer Assistance Fund for underprivileged consumers
Protect consumers through mandatory service standards and compensation

consumer_reality.privatisation_delay: Privatisation not completed until November 2013 — 8 years after enactment
consumer_reality.rural_electrification: REA not operational until approximately 2015 — 10 years after enactment
consumer_reality.tariff_reform: MYTO not operational until 2008; disputed through mid-2010s
consumer_reality.gas_supply: Act silent on gas supply — root cause of generation collapse unaddressed
consumer_reality.captive_generation: Sub-1MW exemption institutionalised the private generator economy
consumer_reality.consumer_protection: Standards published; enforcement negligible; outages continued unaddressed

key_quantitative_targets.successor_companies_formation: Within 8 months of holding company formation
key_quantitative_targets.asset_transfer_to_successors: Within 1 year of initial transfer date
key_quantitative_targets.interim_licence_validity: Maximum 18 months for holding company; 1 year for successor companies

notable_finding: The EPSRA 2005 built a legally sound framework for sector reform but contained a fatal structural omission: no gas supply mandate. Nigeria's gas-dependent generation sector remained crippled by pipeline vandalism, unprofitable gas contracts, and absent upstream-to-power linkages. The Act also institutionalised the two-tier electricity system via the captive generation exemption. Legal architecture was complete by 2005; consumer benefit did not follow for 8-13 years.
targets_met: NERC established as independent regulatory body (2005-2006)
Successor companies eventually formed and privatised (2013)
targets_missed: Competitive market declaration (conditions not met within any foreseeable timeframe post-2005)
Gas supply security (not addressed at all)
links_to_next: REMP 2005 must address rural electrification implementation that EPSRA only legislated
2008 gas flaring deadline (from NEP 2003) still unmet at time of EPSRA passage — gas-to-power gap deepens
10,000 MW by 2007 target (from NEEDS 2004) now structurally impossible — no privatised GENCOs, no investment framework yet operational
Captive generation exemption sets up the generator economy that subsequent documents will need to price or replace
MYTO development becomes the next measurable policy commitment to track
